1: Jehoshaphat . . . strengthened himself against Israel --The temper and proceedings of the kings of Israel rendered it necessary for him to prepare vigorous measures of defense on the northern frontier of his kingdom. These consisted in filling all the fortresses with their full complement of troops and establishing military stations in various parts of the country, as well as in the cities of Mount Ephraim, which belonged to Jehoshaphat (2Ch 15:8). (Commentary by ROBERT JAMIESON)
3: and sought not unto Baalim --a term used for idols generally in contradistinction to the Lord God of his father. (Commentary by ROBERT JAMIESON)
4: "Abraham is said to have kept the commandments and ordinances of God. (Gen. 26:5.) The same is said of Jehoshaphat. (2 Chron. 17:4.) In Hebrews 9:1, 'ordinances' means the requirements of the ceremonial law, as distinct from 'Commandments,' which refers to moral precepts. The ordinances of the gospel are, more especially, baptism, the laying on of hands for the reception of the Holy Spirit, the sacrament, and the rules and regulations of the Church given by divine inspiration. He who is sent of God obeys God's ordinances, as well as His commandments." (Smith and Sjodahl, DCC, p. 306.) (Daniel H. Ludlow, A

and not after the doings of Israel --He observed with scrupulous fidelity, and employed his royal influence to support the divine institutions as enacted by Moses, abhorring that spurious and unlawful calf-worship that now formed the established religion in Israel. Being thus far removed, alike from gross idolatry and Israelitish apostasy, and adhering zealously to the requirements of the divine law, the blessing of God rested on his government. Ruling in the fear of God, and for the good of his subjects, "the Lord established the kingdom in his hand." (Commentary by ROBERT JAMIESON)
Companion to Your Study of the New Testament: The Four Gospels, p.196)
7: Who were "his own" who "received him not"? Were they the Jews in general? Or were they the Qumran community? Jesus fulfilled all of the prophecies that were to be fulfilled by the Messiah except those which would be accomplished at his second coming. It is clear, however, that a great number of people rejected him for not having immediately fulfilled the others also. The main stumbling block to the Essenes would have been their unique reliance on the Aaronic Priesthood, knowing not that the keys of the Melchizedek Priesthood would be restored through the Messiah. And yet, as we have already seen, in the ancient times, especially under kings David and Solomon and the prophet Moses before them, the Melchizedek Priesthood did exist upon the earth and governed the affairs of the Church. The Essenes would have had a tendency to forget that the Aaronic Priesthood is only a division or an incomplete part of the Holy Priesthood after the Order of the Son of God. Had they understood the full meaning of the scriptures, they would have seen how in ancient days the holders of the two types of priesthood worked hand-in-hand. One possible example of this is in 2 Chronicles 17:7-9: Also in the third year of (Jehoshaphat's) reign he sent his princes, even to Ben-hail, and to Obadiah, and to Zechariah, and to Nethaneel, and to Michaiah, to teach in the cities of Judah. And with them he sent Levites, even Shemaiah, and Nethaniah, and Zebadiah, and Asahel, and Shemiramoth, and Jehonathan, and Adonijah, and Tobijah, and Tobadonijah, Levites; and with them Elishama and Jehoram, priests. And they taught in Judah, and had the book of the law of the Lord with them, and went about throughout all the cities of Judah, and taught the people. (John A. Tvedtnes, The Church of the Old Testament, p.72)

Also in the third year of his reign he sent to his princes, . . . to teach in the cities of Judah--The ordinary work of teaching devolved on the priests. But extraordinary commissioners were appointed, probably to ascertain whether the work had been done or neglected. This deputation of five princes, assisted by two priests and nine Levites, was to make a circuit of the towns in Judah. It is the first practical measure we read of as being adopted by any of the kings for the religious instruction of the people. Time and unbroken opportunities were afforded for carrying out fully this excellent plan of home education, for the kingdom enjoyed internal tranquillity as well as freedom for foreign wars. It is conformable to the pious
style of the sacred historian to trace this profound peace to the "fear of the Lord having fallen on all kingdoms of the lands that were round about Judah." (Commentary by ROBERT JAMIESON)
11: the Arabians --the nomad tribes on the south of the Dead Sea, who, seeking the protection of Jehoshaphat after his conquest of Edom, paid their tribute in the way most suitable to their pastoral habits--the present of so many head of cattle. (Commentary by ROBERT JAMIESON)
14: these are the numbers --The warriors were arranged in the army according to their fathers houses. The army of Jehoshaphat, commanded by five great generals and consisting of five unequal divisions, comprised one million one hundred and sixty thousand men, without including those who garrisoned the fortresses. No monarch, since the time of Solomon, equalled Jehoshaphat in the extent of his revenue, in the strength of his fortifications, and in the number of his troops. (Commentary by ROBERT JAMIESON)
